topics.headline: California May Revision FY26-27 — what changed for EL services and LCFF supplemental/concentration
topics.why_matters: Every California partner — all 23 Tier A schools — builds their intervention budget around LCFF supplemental/concentration plus whatever EL-specific lines the state holds steady or cuts. The May Revise is the single most consequential state funding document of the spring. If the Governor protected EL services while cutting elsewhere, that's a story A+ can tell partners. If EL took a haircut, that's a different (and more urgent) story for the LTEL pitch. Either way, Danielle's partner calls next week will reference this document; A+ should be the firm that already framed it on LinkedIn.
topics.angle: "What the May Revise actually changed for charter intervention budgets — three line items every program director should re-check this week." Lead with the EL line, then LCFF, then any intervention-adjacent grant. Practical, not partisan.
topics.roman_take: Conviction angle on why categorical EL funding still matters even when the easier political move is to fold it into LCFF — the LTEL data shows that broad funding without targeted accountability has produced 20+ years of unreclassified students.
topics.danielle_take: Implementation angle on the specific worksheet/budget revision step a charter business officer needs to take in the next 30 days to reflect the May Revise before LCAP adoption season — concrete, calendar-anchored, operator-to-operator.

topics.headline: Nerdy Q1 2026 earnings confirm the platform-tutoring B2B retreat — districts are quietly walking away from on-demand contracts
topics.category: D (Industry/competitor moves) — **strongest competitive intel candidate this cycle**
topics.why_matters: This is the third consecutive quarter where the largest publicly-traded competitor in the K-12 tutoring space has reported B2B/Institutional revenue contraction while leadership pivots messaging toward AI products. The pattern — Paper (2023 layoffs), Tutor.com (district book shrinking through 2024), now Nerdy — is the same story: on-demand, platform-scale tutoring doesn't produce the measurable intervention outcomes districts need to defend Title I and Title III spending. A+'s exact opposite model (scheduled, relationship-based, ESSA-aligned, California-LTEL specialized) is the story districts are arriving at the hard way. A+ has earned the right to comment because A+ has been doing this model the whole time, not pivoting to it after a failed exit.
topics.angle: "The platform-tutoring thesis is failing in public. Here's what California charter leaders are choosing instead — and the three things they're insisting on in 2026-27 contracts."
topics.roman_take: Conviction post on why "scale" was always the wrong KPI for intervention — districts don't buy hours, they buy reclassification rates and EL outcomes, and no platform can sell those because no platform can be accountable for them.
topics.danielle_take: Implementation post on what she's actually hearing in May 2026 conversations with charter directors who are exiting platform contracts — the specific operational gaps (scheduling, progress monitoring, EL specificity) that surface when the platform tries to deliver Tier 2/3 intervention.
